Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic most of the time. I'd try to set aside time on weekends but with work and other responsibilities, it rarely happened as planned. I'm someone who learns best by doing things with my hands, so I'd often spend time practicing concepts physically or making models rather than just reading. My 17 hours of study were scattered throughout the week, usually in bursts when I found pockets of free time between everything else going on.
Q2: My pattern varied wildly depending on what was due and what was happening in my life. I'd have weeks where I barely touched anything, then cram sessions where I'd study for hours straight. Deadlines were definitely the main driver of when I'd actually sit down and focus. I tried to be more consistent but with limited resources and a busy schedule, regular study times felt like a luxury I couldn't afford.
Q3: Usually it was because an assignment was due soon or I needed to check if there were any announcements I'd missed. Sometimes I'd log in just to watch a lecture video while eating dinner or during a break. The discussion posts were another reason since I actually enjoyed connecting with other students there. I'd also check in when I felt guilty about being absent from class and wanted to catch up.
Q4: Yeah, after a few weeks I got into a habit of checking the LMS on my phone during my commute or lunch breaks. It became almost automatic to scroll through and see what was new. The notifications helped remind me to log in regularly. I think once I realized how much I was missing by not checking consistently, it became more of a daily habit even if just for a few minutes.
Q5: The video lectures were honestly lifesavers for me since I could watch them multiple times and pause when I needed to work through something hands-on. I also found the discussion forums really valuable because hearing other students' perspectives helped concepts click for me. The practice quizzes were useful too since I could test myself and see where my gaps were. Anything interactive or that let me engage actively rather than just passively read was what worked best for my learning style.
Q6: I barely touched the supplementary readings and PDFs they uploaded. With limited resources, I couldn't always print them out, and reading long documents on a screen just doesn't work for me. The optional textbook chapters went mostly ignored because I couldn't afford the textbook and the library copies were always checked out. I'd skim them once and then never go back because sitting still and reading isn't how I learn best.
Q7: If I could see how it directly connected to an assignment or exam, I'd engage with it fully. Activities that let me do something rather than just consume information felt worthwhile. I also valued anything that helped me understand concepts I was struggling with since my exam scores mattered a lot to me. If it felt like busy work with no clear purpose, I'd probably skip it or do the bare minimum.
Q8: Optional activities always felt like a trap to me, like they were testing whether I had time I didn't actually have. Required ones I'd prioritize even when stressed, but optional ones got pushed aside when life got busy. Sometimes the optional stuff was actually more interesting, but I couldn't justify spending time on it when I had required work piling up. There was always this guilt about not doing them though.
Q9: Absolutely, a lot of my studying happened offline. I'd download videos and watch them without internet when my connection was unreliable. I made physical flashcards and study aids that I'd carry around with me everywhere. I also studied with classmates in person, working through problems together on whiteboards. A lot of my kinesthetic learning happened with paper, pens, and physical manipulation of concepts that wouldn't show up anywhere online.
Q10: When I was building models or diagrams to understand concepts, none of that showed in the LMS. I'd spend hours working through problems by hand in notebooks, testing myself with flashcards, or explaining concepts out loud to myself while pacing around. Group study sessions at a friend's place where we'd quiz each other were hugely valuable but completely invisible. My best learning often happened away from any screen.
Q11: All the time, yeah. I'd download a lecture video once and watch it three or four more times offline over the next week. Same with any PDFs I did use, I'd save them to my phone and review them during commutes. My internet isn't always reliable, so downloading things once and studying them repeatedly offline became my standard approach. The LMS would only show that single initial access even though I engaged with the material multiple times.
Q12: Around midterms there was a period where everything felt overwhelming. I was dealing with some personal stuff, my motivation dipped, and attending classes felt pointless. The CBT sessions I did actually helped me work through some of that mental fog. There were also weeks when work demanded more hours and I just couldn't keep up with everything. Those were the times my attendance really suffered.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted from work or stressed about money, the LMS became something I'd avoid because it reminded me of everything I was behind on. Confusion about certain topics made me procrastinate on related materials since facing what I didn't understand felt harder than ignoring it. Competing responsibilities meant I'd often do the minimum to get by rather than engaging deeply like I wanted to.
Q14: There were plenty of times I'd have a video playing but my mind was elsewhere, worrying about bills or just zoning out from fatigue. I'd scroll through discussion posts without really absorbing anything. Sometimes I'd open materials just to feel like I was doing something productive, but I wasn't actually learning. Those sessions felt hollow even though they'd show up as engagement in the system.
Q15: I'd postpone the longer written assignments because they felt so daunting when I was already tired. Even knowing they were important, I'd convince myself I'd do them tomorrow when I had more energy. Some readings I avoided because they were dense and I knew I'd struggle to focus on them. The irony is the avoidance made the stress worse, which I worked through in my CBT sessions.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly become incredibly productive. My LMS activity probably spiked dramatically in the days before anything was due. Without deadlines, I'd drift and tell myself I had time. It wasn't healthy but it's how I functioned given everything else competing for my attention.
Q17: When I got good feedback, it motivated me to engage more with similar activities. Positive comments on discussion posts made me want to participate more. But when feedback was delayed or vague, I'd lose momentum and not know if I was on the right track. The feedback on my exam scores was really encouraging and pushed me to keep putting in effort even when attendance was hard.
Q18: The course structure with clear modules helped me know what to focus on each week. Having videos, discussions, and assignments grouped together made it easier to see what was expected. But the flexibility also meant I could fall behind easily since there wasn't much forcing me to stay on track between major deadlines. I appreciated the structure but sometimes needed more external accountability.
Q19: The video format worked really well for my learning style since I could pause and practice along. The discussion requirements actually helped because they forced me to engage with peers, which I enjoyed. What made it harder was when materials were only available as long text documents with no interactive elements. Also, when too many things were due in the same week, it became overwhelming to manage.
Q20: I think some students might be searching for understanding but struggling to produce work that meets standards. They could be engaging with materials trying to learn but hitting walls when it comes to actually completing assignments. Or maybe they're dealing with anxiety about submitting work that isn't perfect. High activity could also mean someone is lost and clicking around trying to figure things out without success.
Q21: That actually sounds a lot like me in some ways. I performed well on exams despite not having the highest LMS activity because I studied effectively offline. Some people just learn differently and don't need to be constantly logged in to understand material. If you already grasp concepts through other means like hands-on practice or peer discussion, you might not need to revisit online materials as much.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when someone is active, what they click on, and whether they complete tasks. But they miss the quality of engagement, whether someone is actually thinking deeply or just going through motions. They can't capture offline studying, emotional state, or whether life circumstances are affecting someone's ability to engage. The numbers tell part of the story but not the whole picture.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just getting through it. It's when I'm curious and want to know more, when I'm connecting concepts to real life or other things I know. For me, engagement feels active, like I'm doing something with the knowledge rather than just receiving it. It's also about feeling connected to the course community and invested in my own progress.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how much I studied with physical materials, the flashcards I made, or the models I built to understand concepts. It wouldn't capture the peer study sessions or the times I taught concepts to others to solidify my own understanding. It also couldn't show my emotional journey, how the CBT sessions helped me manage stress and stay motivated despite challenges. My engagement was much richer than any log could capture.
